name: Cova de ses Mosques Grosses
description: A structurally-originated cave on the SE slope of Puig Gros de Bendinat with a small entrance and low ceiling requiring crawling. It features two main chambers: an entry hall with abundant lithochemical formations and corrosion cupolas, and a larger hall with a beautiful flowstone descending to -17.5 m depth. The cave continues beyond the current obstruction but is not yet passable.
full_description:
Description.

On the SE slope of Puig Gros de Bendinat (486 m), just below the path of the same name and at the base of a small escarpment, lies this cavity of structural origin, with a small entrance and low ceiling.

Entrance Hall

From the very first moment we enter the cavity, we must crawl along a ramp with a gradient close to 25% in the W-E direction. With dimensions of 6 x 6 x 1.5 m, lithochemical processes abound in the hall, while in the ceiling zone a couple of corrosion cupolas can be appreciated, perhaps the result of thermal aggressions within the cavity; one of the cupolas is filled with delicate speleothems.

The ceiling of the hall at some points does not reach half a meter in height. Seven meters further down, we find ourselves at what appears to be the end of the cavity, but it is nothing other than an obstruction due to the wedging of large stone blocks, where clearing work had to be carried out to continue the exploration.

Flowstone Hall

To enter this hall, one must descend from the clearing zone through unstable materials, making this a somewhat dangerous area due to rockfall from stones that have accumulated, dragged in from outside the cave. It is recommended to install a rope on the exterior of the cavity to help overcome this passage. A kind of channel on the west indicates the descent, while the cave opens up to the east, and a beautiful flowstone descends from the south to reach -17.5 m, this being the deepest zone of the hall. In reality it is a new obstruction that prevents further progress, although we have been able to verify that the cave continues descending for quite a few more meters. The easternmost surface of the hall is carpeted with calcium carbonate from the aforementioned flowstone; in this area a considerable number of speleothems abound.

Visiting this cavity does not require a full day, so it can be complemented by visiting some of the caves found in the vicinity.
